summary: Charity Majors argues against mandating pronoun sharing at work while supporting the normalization and safety of voluntary disclosure. Drawing from her own experience as a queer person with complicated feelings about gender identity, she explains how forced pronoun sharing can backfire by pressuring people who are questioning or transitioning, priming gender bias, and eliminating useful signal about genuine allyship.
key_points: Making pronoun sharing safe and normalized is good, but mandating it is counterproductive
Forced pronoun disclosure pressures people who are questioning, transitioning, or not yet out
Research suggests priming people to think of gender first can activate bias about competence
Mandates flatten useful social signal about who is genuinely a safe ally
Rules are easier to enforce than winning hearts and minds, but backlash is real
money_quotes: The strongest feeling I have about my gender is apathy/discomfort/irritation. Maybe that will change someday, maybe it won't, but I resent being forced to pick a side and make some kind of public declaration when I'm just trying to do my goddamn job.
Mandating it means we can't look around the room and determine who might be friendly or safe, while also escalating resentment that bigots hold towards us.

How much easier it is to make and enforce rules, if you're in a position with the power to do so, than to win hearts and minds. Rules always have edge cases and unintended consequences, and the backlash effect is real. People don't like being told what to do.
Research shows that priming someone to think of you as a woman first and foremost generally leads them to think of you as being less technical, less authoritative, even less competent.
key_insight: Well-intentioned pronoun mandates at work can backfire by pressuring vulnerable people, activating gender bias, and destroying the very social signals that help queer people identify genuine allies.
